title: Hidden Voids Discovered in Menkaure Pyramid Suggest Secret Entrance
summary: Researchers from Cairo University and the Technical University of Munich, working on the ScanPyramids project, have discovered two air-filled cavities behind the eastern facade of the Menkaure pyramid at Giza. The eastern side has long puzzled scientists because a section of granite blocks is unusually smooth and polished, similar to the known entrance on the north side. In 2019, researcher Stijn van den Hoven proposed that a second entrance might exist there. Using non-invasive techniques such as ground-penetrating radar, ultrasound, and electrical resistivity tomography, the team confirmed two voids at depths of 1.4 and 1.13 meters behind the outer wall. One void measures about 1 meter high and 1.5 meters wide, the other 0.9 by 0.7 meters. The data integration method called Image Fusion helped validate the findings. This discovery strengthens the hypothesis of a hidden entrance on the east side. Professor Christian Grosse from TUM noted that the methodology allows precise conclusions without damaging the structure. The ScanPyramids project previously found a hidden corridor in the Great Pyramid in 2023.

background_read: The Menkaure pyramid is the third largest pyramid at Giza, built around 2510 BC for Pharaoh Menkaure. The ScanPyramids project uses modern technology to explore the pyramids without damage. In 2023, they discovered a hidden corridor in the Great Pyramid of Khufu using muon radiography. The new findings at Menkaure suggest that there may be a second entrance, which could provide insights into pyramid construction and burial practices.
Article_Structure: The article reports the discovery of two voids behind the eastern facade of the Menkaure pyramid, supporting the hypothesis of a secret entrance. Main points: the voids were detected using radar, ultrasound, and ERT; the location corresponds to a polished granite section; and the findings are part of the ScanPyramids project. Purpose: to inform about a significant archaeological discovery. Evidence evaluation: the use of multiple non-invasive techniques and image fusion provides strong evidence. Author credibility: the research involves reputable institutions (Cairo University, TUM). Methodology: ground-penetrating radar, ultrasound, and electrical resistivity tomography were combined to confirm the voids.

perspectives.description: The discovery could reveal a new entrance, changing our understanding of pyramid access.
The use of non-invasive methods demonstrates how modern technology can uncover ancient secrets.
